demand.explanation: Gold-medal final with $553.99 price ceiling and Fox as the marquee contender. Her Paris double and public statements to defend her titles drive premium demand; this ranks among the most sought-after Games sessions in the canoe slalom program.

scorecard.overall: A strong gold-medal final carried by Jessica Fox's bid to repeat her Paris double in kayak and canoe. Morning timing and 500-person venue limit pure energy against larger evening finals, but Fox's star power, medal stakes, and specialized venue make this a high-demand Games session.

significance.explanation: Gold-medal final determining the men's canoe single champion through one-run semifinal and final. Nicolas Gestin, Paris gold medalist with stated intent to defend, leads the field in one of six slalom medal events at the Games.

experience.explanation: Morning medal final at OKC Whitewater Center with covered patios and elevated deck viewing of rapids and technical gates. Viewing quality is strong from a purpose-built venue; morning timing limits peak-energy atmosphere compared to evening finals despite high-stakes competition.

starPower.explanation: Nicolas Gestin, Paris gold medalist, is confirmed as the favorites with publicly stated intent to defend his title. Leading world rankings, he carries marquee status within slalom; top men's paddlers are recognized Games competitors with significant sporting prominence.

uniqueness.explanation: Gold-medal final of men's canoe single anchored by Gestin's defense of his Paris title. The format is established, but a defending champion pursuing back-to-back golds in a marquee discipline adds compelling narrative weight to this medal session.

demand.explanation: Gold-medal final with $553.99 price ceiling and Gestin as the marquee contender. Defending-champion storyline and world-ranked status drive strong demand; this ranks among sought-after medal sessions in the slalom program despite smaller venue size.

scorecard.overall: A strong gold-medal final centered on Nicolas Gestin's bid to defend his Paris canoe single title. Morning timing and specialized 500-capacity venue trade against peak energy, but Gestin's Paris success, medal stakes, and defending-champion narrative make this a compelling Games ticket.

significance.explanation: Preliminary round introducing kayak cross, a head-to-head format that filters the field through round 1 and repechage. Paddlers compete for advancement; stakes are earned through qualification rather than medals, typical of early-round competition.

experience.explanation: Morning preliminary of kayak cross, a full-contact head-to-head format with inherent entertainment value — boats collide, paddlers tangle, Eskimo rolls add spectacle. Venue wraps spectators close to action; morning slot limits energy, but the sport's collision-heavy format compensates.

starPower.explanation: Jessica Fox and Matej Benus, Paris kayak cross gold medalists, could appear if they qualify from heats. Early-round formats often feature top athletes, though appearance is not guaranteed; kayak cross attracts a strong field of world-class paddlers.

uniqueness.explanation: Preliminary round of kayak cross, a returning head-to-head format reintroduced from Paris 2024. Round 1 and repechage are early-stage heats; the format itself carries novelty within slalom, though this is a standard preliminary structure.

demand.explanation: Preliminary kayak cross session with lower price point ($37.84–$290.51). Early-round sessions track easier to book; kayak cross's high-energy format may drive above-baseline interest relative to standard slalom prelims, but medal-round demand still exceeds prelims.

scorecard.overall: A preliminary kayak cross session with inherent entertainment from full-contact boat racing. Morning timing and prelim format constrain demand relative to finals, though the format's collision-heavy chaos and possible appearance of Paris medalists provide solid preliminary interest.

significance.explanation: Preliminary heats in kayak cross advancing paddlers toward quarterfinals. Competitors earn advancement through qualification rather than medals; stakes are real for individual paddlers but fall short of medal-deciding rounds.

experience.explanation: Morning kayak cross heats featuring the full-contact head-to-head format — four-boat mass-start races with collisions, tactical blocking, and jostling through gates. Venue proximity and sport's inherent chaos deliver good live entertainment despite morning timing and shorter duration (9:00–11:00).

starPower.explanation: Paris medalists surviving round 1 and repechage likely advance to heats; top kayak cross paddlers are expected. Early-round sessions routinely feature world-class athletes, though not all marquee names are guaranteed to appear in this specific prelim round.

uniqueness.explanation: Preliminary heats of kayak cross, a continuing head-to-head format from Paris 2024. Heats follow round 1 in a standard progression; this is a typical prelim session in an established sport rather than a once-in-a-lifetime pairing.

demand.explanation: Preliminary kayak cross heats with lower price ceiling ($37.84–$290.51) and shorter session duration. Prelim sessions track easier to book relative to medal rounds; kayak cross's entertainment value may moderate this slightly, but demand remains baseline for early-round competition.

scorecard.overall: A preliminary kayak cross session with strong sport-specific entertainment from full-contact racing. Morning timing, short duration, and prelim format limit demand relative to finals, though the format's collision-heavy chaos and expected top-paddler presence provide solid preliminary interest.

significance.explanation: This session crowns both men's and women's kayak cross champions through quarterfinals, semifinals, and finals in one morning, delivering two gold medals and closing the entire six-medal slalom program. Medal stakes combined with double-gold format create peak significance.

experience.explanation: Morning final of kayak cross — full-contact head-to-head racing with boats colliding, tactical blocking, and dramatic four-paddler sprints. Closing session energy, compact quarterfinal-to-final format, and sport's inherent chaos deliver high live entertainment and spectator intensity despite morning timing.

starPower.explanation: Jessica Fox, Paris women's kayak cross gold medalist and public favorite, pursues another title. Finlay Williamson, Paris men's silver medalist with strong 2025-2026 form, headlines the men's field. This is the highest-profile kayak cross session of the Games with marquee defending champions and clear contenders.

uniqueness.explanation: Gold-medal finals in both men's and women's kayak cross on the closing day of the canoe slalom program. Double-medal crowning at the season's finale, with Fox chasing another title in the full-contact format, creates a distinct once-every-four-years closing event.

demand.explanation: Gold-medal finals (x2) with $553.99 price ceiling on the final slalom competition day. Fox's pursuit of another kayak cross title, closing-session positioning, and double-medal format drive premium demand; this ranks among the most sought-after sessions in the entire slalom program.

scorecard.overall: The closing double-gold-medal finale of canoe slalom, delivering marquee entertainment through kayak cross quarterfinals, semifinals, and finals. Jessica Fox's bid for another title and the full-contact format's chaos combine with closing-day energy to make this the highest-demand slalom session despite morning timing.

significance.explanation: Heats and quarterfinals carry real advancement stakes for 236 athletes competing across four events, but medal decisions are still rounds away. Early-round progression in Canoe Sprint establishes the field rather than crowning champions.

experience.explanation: Marine Stadium's 1932-built shoreline setting and calm flatwater deliver close-range sightlines, though the 9 AM start time limits the prime-time energy that evening sessions would provide. Multiple back-to-back heats offer continuous action despite the morning slot.

starPower.explanation: World-class paddlers from major programs appear in heats and quarterfinals, but top athletes in Canoe Sprint are not globally household names. Early rounds may feature Olympic medalists and world champions, though not necessarily the highest-profile entries.

uniqueness.explanation: Opening-round sessions follow a standard format across multiple events at the venue; while Marine Stadium's history and setting carry character, this quarterfinal session is a routine progression round rather than a once-in-a-lifetime pairing of sport and venue.

demand.explanation: Early-round sessions with a modest $28–$285 price ceiling and no medal stakes track lower relative to finals, though Games scarcity and the historic Marine Stadium setting mean even preliminary racing remains sought-after compared to non-Games events.

scorecard.overall: CSP01 is a baseline 2028 Games session that opens Canoe Sprint with solid venue character and continuous action, but lacks medal stakes and the prime-time slot that would elevate its profile. It serves as a strong entry point for paddling fans and venue enthusiasts rather than a marquee draw.

significance.explanation: Four gold medals are awarded across men's and women's K2 500m and men's C1 1000m, placing this session among the most medal-rich Canoe Sprint events. Medal finals carry the highest stakes in the sport, crowning four separate champions.

experience.explanation: Medal finals on a historic flatwater course deliver high-stakes drama and podium ceremonies, though the 9 AM morning slot limits the evening prime-time energy that peak-demand sessions generate. Marine Stadium's shoreline seating and close sightlines enhance the finish-line tension across four races.

starPower.explanation: Medal finals attract the top contenders in men's and women's K2 500m and men's C1 1000m, including world champions and medalists from prior Games. While Canoe Sprint lacks globally recognizable superstar names, finalists represent the sport's elite tier.

uniqueness.explanation: Medal finals in Canoe Sprint at a historic 1932 venue carry special weight, and the specific pairing of four gold-medal races in a single morning creates a concentrated championship moment. However, medal finals themselves are a standard Games format, not a once-in-a-lifetime venue-sport combination.

demand.explanation: Medal-round pricing ($74–$508) and four-gold-medal slate position this session well above early-round sessions. The morning slot and non-marquee sport status prevent it from reaching top-tier demand, but Games scarcity and medal stakes drive strong ticket interest.

scorecard.overall: CSP02 is a strong medal-round session delivering four gold medals in rapid succession, elevated by Marine Stadium's intimate setting and historical significance. The morning timing and early-season placement limit it from peak demand, but this remains a marquee Canoe Sprint morning with high competitive stakes.

significance.explanation: Heats and quarterfinals across six events determine advancement but do not decide medals. This quarterfinal session carries the advancement stakes inherent to early rounds, particularly notable for hosting men's K1 1000m and women's K1 500m debuts at the 2028 Games.

experience.explanation: Six events packed into a single morning generate continuous back-to-back action and high throughput on the Marine Stadium course, keeping sightlines busy. The 9 AM–1:30 PM window extends the session length, though it straddles morning and early afternoon rather than capturing full prime-time energy.

starPower.explanation: Early-round sessions draw world-class competitors from medal programs, but the broader field includes regional qualifiers alongside elite paddlers. Top stars may not feature prominently in every heat, particularly in the larger team-boat divisions.

uniqueness.explanation: While this session introduces the reformatted men's K1 1000m and women's K1 500m distance events, it remains an early-round progression session following standard Games structure. The novelty of distance changes is outweighed by the routine format of quarterfinal racing.

demand.explanation: Six-event quarterfinal sessions with a $28–$285 price range and no medals track lower on demand than medal finals. Extended morning duration may attract fans seeking comprehensive Canoe Sprint immersion, but this remains among the more accessible sessions for booking.

scorecard.overall: CSP03 is the most voluminous Canoe Sprint session, offering six events and continuous shoreline action across a long Thursday morning. It appeals most to dedicated sport enthusiasts and those seeking total Canoe Sprint immersion rather than those targeting specific marquee moments or medal ceremonies.

significance.explanation: Three gold medals are awarded in women's K1 500m, women's C2 500m, and men's K1 1000m, making this a premier medal-round session. These include the women's K1 500m, described as among the sport's most prestigious races and now featuring the Games-reformatted 500m distance.

experience.explanation: Medal finals under Marine Stadium's intimate shoreline setting deliver close-range finish-line drama and three separate podium ceremonies. The 9 AM–12:40 PM timing places this in late morning rather than prime evening, which moderates spectator energy relative to what an evening slot would generate.

starPower.explanation: Medal finals in the women's K1 500m (described as prestigious), women's C2 500m, and men's K1 1000m attract world champions and top international contenders. While Canoe Sprint lacks superstar household names, finalists in these marquee events represent the sport's elite.

uniqueness.explanation: Medal finals at Marine Stadium carry weight and ceremony, elevated further by the women's K1 500m's prestige and the men's K1 1000m's Games-reformatted distance. However, medal finals are a standard format; the session is notable for sport-specific prestige rather than once-in-a-lifetime venue-sport pairing.

demand.explanation: Medal-round pricing ($74–$508) and three-gold-medal slate position this session as a top-tier draw within Canoe Sprint. The women's K1 500m prestige and morning timing on a Friday create strong ticket demand, though the non-marquee sport status and lack of global superstar appeal keep it below peak Games demand.

scorecard.overall: CSP04 is a strong medal-round session anchored by the prestigious women's K1 500m and rounded out by two other gold-medal finals. Marine Stadium's close-range setting and three podium ceremonies elevate the experience, though the late-morning slot and sport's niche global profile prevent it from reaching peak marquee status.

significance.explanation: Four gold medals conclude the entire Canoe Sprint program on Saturday morning, encompassing the K4 500m team spectacles and the explosive women's C1 200m and men's C2 500m. This is the championship-concluding session with the full complement of final medals for the discipline.

experience.explanation: The K4 races showcase pure power and synchronized team paddling, while the women's C1 200m delivers sub-minute explosive intensity. Marine Stadium's coastal setting and back-to-back medal finals with multiple podium ceremonies create a festive closing-day atmosphere, though the 9 AM start limits evening prime-time energy.

starPower.explanation: Medal finals attract the top national programs and world-class team boats in K4, plus elite solo and doubles canoe specialists. Canoe Sprint's lack of global superstars means medal-round finalists are accomplished athletes within the sport rather than household names.

uniqueness.explanation: The final Canoe Sprint medals at Marine Stadium mark the sport's Games conclusion, and the K4 team boat races are among Canoe Sprint's most visually striking events. This closing-day status and the culmination of the week-long competition provide narrative weight beyond a standard medal round.

demand.explanation: Medal-round pricing ($74–$508), four gold medals, and the sport's final morning position drive strong demand. Saturday close-out status and the K4 spectacle elevate ticket interest, though the non-marquee sport status and morning slot keep demand below the Games' absolute peak.

scorecard.overall: CSP05 is Canoe Sprint's championship finale, delivering four gold medals with the K4 team races as the headliners. Marine Stadium's historic venue and coastal setting provide a fitting close to the sport, and the closing-day narrative significance pushes this above typical medal sessions despite the late-morning timing.

significance.explanation: Qualifying rounds establish seeding but do not award medals. These heats determine advancement for afternoon medal finals, making them meaningful for athletes even though medals are not at stake.

experience.explanation: A 9:00 AM morning session at an indoor velodrome with excellent sightlines, but the early hour and preliminary format lack the prime-time energy of evening medal rounds. The climate-controlled venue and speed events provide solid spectator engagement despite timing.

starPower.explanation: Defending champions from Netherlands and Great Britain will likely compete in qualifying, and top-seeded teams usually field their strongest rosters even in prelims. World-class sprinters are present, though the session does not guarantee marquee-name matchups.

uniqueness.explanation: Team sprint qualifying is a standard preliminary round repeated across many track cycling events. While qualifying sets the stage for afternoon drama, this session itself follows a routine format without distinctive pairings or historical significance.

demand.explanation: Entry-level pricing and morning timing make this session more accessible than afternoon medal rounds. Games scarcity drives interest, but qualifying sessions typically attract smaller crowds than finals, making tickets easier to acquire relative to other track events.

scorecard.overall: An early-morning qualifying session that establishes the day's medal brackets but lacks the stakes and prime-time appeal of finals. Solid viewing experience and accessible pricing make it a reasonable entry point to track cycling, though spectators seeking marquee racing will find more urgency in afternoon sessions.

significance.explanation: Two team sprint finals (men's and women's) award gold medals back-to-back, plus men's team pursuit qualifying launches a second major event. This session decides medals in two medal events—peak significance for a track cycling session.

experience.explanation: Midday final sessions with medal ceremonies create sustained energy and excitement. The three-hour window packs multiple finals plus opening drama, and team sprint's explosive 50+ mph bursts on the banking deliver visceral spectacle in an intimate 2,450-seat venue.

starPower.explanation: Netherlands men and Great Britain women enter as defending champions and will feature their best sprinters. Supporting teams (Australia, New Zealand, Germany) field world-class riders. Multiple gold-medal-caliber athletes guaranteed on the track.

uniqueness.explanation: Team sprint finals paired with men's team pursuit qualifying create a rare combination of medal rounds from two disciplines in one session. The opening medal races for 2028 track cycling carry narrative weight as defending champions face challengers.

demand.explanation: Two gold medals and midday timing attract strong ticket demand. Price ceiling of $558 reflects premium interest. Games scarcity plus medal stakes position this among the most sought-after track cycling sessions.

scorecard.overall: A high-stakes session anchored by two team sprint finals featuring defending champions, plus the launch of men's team pursuit competition. Midday timing and explosive racing style generate peak energy and viewership, making this one of the marquee days of the track cycling program.

significance.explanation: The session spans qualifying, first rounds, and repechages across four disciplines—all advancement rounds without medal-deciding stakes. Women's team pursuit qualifying and men's team pursuit first rounds carry consequence for athletes, though medals are determined later.

experience.explanation: A long morning block (8:45 AM–12:15 PM) covering diverse disciplines provides variety but lacks prime-time energy. Early timing and preliminary-focused format limit crowd intensity, though the velodrome's climate control and sightlines remain strong for this multi-event marathon.

starPower.explanation: Defending champion Harrie Lavreysen (Netherlands men's sprint gold) will likely compete, alongside defending women's pursuit champions from USA. World-class competitors are certain in all four disciplines, though the blunt of top-tier stars appear in medal rounds later in the program.

uniqueness.explanation: This session combines routine qualifying and first-round heats across multiple events—a standard mid-Games format. The breadth of disciplines adds variety but does not confer special historical or venue-based uniqueness.

demand.explanation: Early morning and preliminary-focused rounds make this session more accessible than medal finals. The low price ceiling ($297.67) and focus on advancement rather than medals position it as a lower-demand session relative to finals, though Games presale still ensures strong interest.

scorecard.overall: A comprehensive morning session that covers qualifying and early rounds across four disciplines, offering breadth rather than concentrated drama. While world-class athletes and the venue are strong, early timing and preliminary format appeal most to committed enthusiasts rather than casual spectators seeking medal drama.

significance.explanation: The men's team pursuit final awards gold and is the session's anchor, while women's keirin quarterfinals advance toward medals and men's sprint rounds continue medal-path competition. One gold medal and two gold-medal-path events create strong significance.

experience.explanation: Afternoon timing (1:45–4:45 PM) aligns with peak spectator energy and natural light, and the men's team pursuit final delivers sustained 4000-meter drama as four-rider squads maintain devastating pace. Sprint tactics and keirin bunch racing add variety to a packed three-hour program.

starPower.explanation: Defending champion Australia enters the men's team pursuit final with strong medalists likely competing. New Zealand's Ellesse Andrews, defending keirin gold medalist, will appear in women's quarterfinals. Multiple Paris 2024 medalists and world-class sprinters create a strong field.

uniqueness.explanation: While the men's team pursuit final is a marquee event, the session combines it with mid-round sprint and keirin competition rather than a once-in-a-lifetime pairing. The pursuit final itself carries significance, though it shares the bill with progression rounds.

demand.explanation: Men's team pursuit final plus afternoon timing generate strong ticket demand. Price ceiling of $558 reflects premium interest in medal rounds during prime hours. Easier to access than evening finals, but still a high-draw session.

scorecard.overall: An afternoon session built around the men's team pursuit final, featuring defending Australian champions defending their 2024 gold. Solid afternoon timing and the appeal of a major medal match drive strong demand, though sprint and keirin rounds provide supporting drama rather than headline moments.

significance.explanation: This session crowns gold medalists in women's team pursuit, women's keirin, and men's sprint finals, plus completes the entire men's omnium (four rounds) to award all-around gold. Four gold medals across four disciplines represent the peak of track cycling's significance.

experience.explanation: Five hours of competition spanning finals and an all-day omnium create sustained, marathon spectator energy. The omnium's variety (scratch race, tempo race, elimination race, points race) contrasts sharply with sprint explosiveness, offering the full spectrum of track racing in a single immersive day.

starPower.explanation: USA women's pursuit team defending their 2024 gold, defending sprint champion Ellesse Andrews (New Zealand) in keirin finals, and men's omnium attracting world-class all-rounders. Harrie Lavreysen may compete across sprint events. Multiple gold-medal contenders guaranteed.

uniqueness.explanation: The omnium running all four rounds in a single day-long session is distinctive, and combining it with three separate finals creates a rare mega-session. The marathon format distinguishes this from typical medal-round pairings.

demand.explanation: Four gold medals and all-day programming command premium demand. Price ceiling of $558 and the sheer density of medal events make this highly sought-after. Sessions packing multiple finals consistently track toward sell-out, especially with afternoon positioning.

scorecard.overall: The crown jewel of track cycling—four gold medals across four disciplines, including the all-day men's omnium and sprint/pursuit/keirin finals. The five-hour window and medal density, combined with defending champions and world-class all-rounders, create the Games' highest concentration of track cycling significance and appeal.

significance.explanation: Men's sprint semifinals narrow the medal field to four finalists (high stakes for athletes), while women's sprint qualifying and early rounds begin medal-path competition. Semifinals carry meaningful advancement pressure, though finals are decided in later sessions.

experience.explanation: A compact morning session (9:00–11:20 AM) with early timing that lacks prime-time energy. The velodrome's sightlines remain excellent and sprint racing's tactical intensity is compelling, but morning slot and two-hour duration limit crowd energy relative to afternoon finals.

starPower.explanation: Defending champion Harrie Lavreysen (Netherlands men's sprint gold) will almost certainly compete in semifinals, alongside silver medalist Matthew Richardson (Australia) and bronze medalist Jack Carlin (Great Britain). Top-tier sprinters guaranteed despite morning timing.

uniqueness.explanation: Men's sprint semifinals are a standard round in the medal progression, and women's sprint qualifying launches a routine preliminary phase. The session follows conventional track cycling format without distinctive venue-sport pairings or historical uniqueness.

demand.explanation: Morning timing and focus on semifinals (rather than finals) make this session less critical than medal rounds. Price ceiling of $365.89 reflects mid-tier demand. The star power of defending champions elevates interest above routine prelims, but morning slot and non-final status limit peak demand.

scorecard.overall: A morning semifinal session where defending sprint champions and top medalists vie for final spots. Strong star power and tactical racing appeal, but early timing and non-medal-deciding status position it as a moderately sought-after session for dedicated track cycling enthusiasts.

significance.explanation: Men's sprint final and women's madison final both award gold medals—two gold medals in a single session, each representing the highest stakes in their respective disciplines. This pairs the signature one-on-one showdown with madison's chaotic team spectacle.

experience.explanation: Afternoon session (1:00–3:15 PM) featuring two distinct racing styles: the minimalist, tactical intensity of men's sprint finals (three laps, best-of-three head-to-head) contrasts sharply with the frenetic chaos of women's madison (120 laps, hand-sling exchanges, sprinting for points). Sustained variety and energy.

starPower.explanation: Men's sprint final features defending champion Harrie Lavreysen (Netherlands) against silver medalist Matthew Richardson (Australia) and bronze medalist Jack Carlin (Great Britain)—a trio of household names in track cycling. Women's madison features defending champions Italy against Great Britain and Netherlands.

uniqueness.explanation: Two gold medals (sprint and madison) at Carson Velodrome in a single afternoon session creates a rare pairing of the sport's most iconic and chaotic events. The sprint final is track cycling's signature showdown; madison is its most distinctive discipline—together they showcase the sport's range.

demand.explanation: Two gold medals with afternoon prime-time positioning and defending champions in both events command premium demand. Price ceiling of $558 reflects high competition. Casual and dedicated spectators alike target this session for marquee racing.

scorecard.overall: The marquee afternoon session pairing two gold medals in contrasting disciplines: the tactical chess of men's sprint finals versus the controlled chaos of women's madison. Defending champions, world-class stars, and the 2:15 PM timing create peak appeal and demand, making this one of the track cycling program's signature events.

significance.explanation: Men's madison final awards gold in one of track cycling's most unique disciplines, while women's sprint knockout rounds (1/8 & QF) advance medal-path competitors. One gold medal plus two gold-path rounds deliver strong significance, though one final limits it from perfect 10.

experience.explanation: Late morning session (11:00 AM–2:35 PM) with afternoon energy momentum building. Men's madison final's 200-lap endurance spectacle of hand-sling exchanges and sprint bursts delivers sustained drama. Women's sprint tactics and men's keirin first-round heats provide supporting variety.

starPower.explanation: Defending champion Portugal men's madison gold medalists will compete, alongside defending women's sprint gold Ellesse Andrews (New Zealand). Supporting medalists from Italy, Germany, and Great Britain bring world-class depth. Strong cast without the mega-star concentration of sprint-final sessions.

uniqueness.explanation: Men's madison final is a marquee event, but the session combines it with women's sprint quarters and men's keirin first round—solid supporting events but not distinctive pairings. The madison itself is a unique discipline, though the overall session follows standard medal-round sequencing.

demand.explanation: One gold medal and late-morning timing (approaching lunch hour and afternoon) attract strong ticket demand. Price ceiling of $558 reflects premium interest in madison final and sprint progression. Expected to track toward sell-out, though single final limits peak demand relative to multi-medal sessions.

scorecard.overall: A solid late-morning session headlined by the men's madison final—Portugal defending their chaotic 200-lap team spectacle against Italy and Denmark. Supporting drama in women's sprint and men's keirin first round adds depth. Strong demand and compelling racing, though single gold medal and lack of sprint-final matchups keep it slightly below the program's peak sessions.

significance.explanation: This final session crowns gold in women's sprint, men's keirin, and women's omnium—three gold medals. The omnium's completion (four rounds) plus two individual finals represent the program's closing apex, with maximum medal density.

experience.explanation: Morning timing (8:45 AM–12:35 PM) lacks prime evening energy, but the four-hour span delivers nonstop competition with shifting focus across three gold-medal events. The omnium's grind (scratch, tempo, elimination, points race) contrasts with sprint and keirin explosiveness, offering comprehensive track cycling climax.

starPower.explanation: Defending women's sprint gold Ellesse Andrews (New Zealand) likely competing in both sprint and keirin finals (potential double-gold contender). Harrie Lavreysen defending keirin gold, Paris medalists from Germany and Great Britain in sprint. World-class all-rounders in omnium. Star-studded closing day.

uniqueness.explanation: The closing day of the 2028 track cycling program awarding three golds (including the full omnium) carries narrative weight as the program's climax. This is the final opportunity to witness Carson Velodrome track cycling at the Games, adding historical significance.

demand.explanation: Three gold medals and the program's closing day drive strong demand among spectators seeking culminating drama. Price ceiling of $558 reflects premium positioning. Morning timing limits casual attendance slightly, but Games presale and the closing-day narrative keep tickets highly sought.

scorecard.overall: The 2028 track cycling program's closing morning session, packing three gold medals (women's sprint, men's keirin, women's omnium) into four hours. Defending champions across disciplines and the omnium's marathon format provide sustained, varied drama. Morning timing and the end-of-program positioning attract dedicated enthusiasts seeking the program's final chapter.

significance.explanation: Preliminary round carries earned stakes for athletes competing for advancement, but lacks medal decisions. Nearly 30 divers competing for 18 semifinal spots means real consequences for execution—miss a dive and the door closes—but significance is limited by the non-medal status of this opening session.

experience.explanation: Morning session (10:00 AM) at an open-air venue limits prime-time energy, though poolside viewing in a parkland setting offers clarity and intimacy. Early start and preliminary-round action create a solid Games experience without the electricity of evening competition.

starPower.explanation: Paris 2024 medalists Chang Ya Wen (Chinese Taipei silver) and Maddison Keeney (Australia bronze) may compete, alongside China's top springboard specialists. The field contains world-class athletes even if not all household names, anchoring the session in elite competition.

uniqueness.explanation: A standard preliminary format of an established sport at a dedicated venue. While this is diving's first separation from swimming at the Games, this particular session is one of multiple qualifying rounds across the program—the novelty lies in the venue arrangement, not in this specific session's structure.

demand.explanation: Early-morning preliminary with a modest price ceiling ($28–$498) makes this the most accessible diving session, though Games scarcity still supports demand. Easier to book relative to finals, but ticket scarcity ensures this is never truly 'easy' to obtain.

scorecard.overall: A baseline Games session—the opening preliminary of the diving program offers poolside viewing at an intimate venue, but morning timing and non-medal stakes limit its draw. Still a live 2028 ticket with world-class athletes, though not a marquee session.

significance.explanation: Semifinal narrows the field from 18 to 12, carrying tangible advancement stakes. One misstep ends a finalist berth, raising the pressure, but without medal implications the significance remains below medal-round competition.

experience.explanation: Mid-morning session (10:00 AM–12:00 PM) at an open-air poolside venue delivers unobstructed sightlines and close proximity to athletes, though the morning slot lacks afternoon warmth and energy. Solid spectator experience hindered slightly by timing rather than venue or sport.

starPower.explanation: The field includes Paris 2024 medalists Maddison Keeney (bronze) and likely other national champions and world-level springboard specialists. Top athletes compete in semifinal rounds even if not all are household names, supporting a credible star presence.

uniqueness.explanation: Semifinal follows a standard format shared across many sessions during the diving program. The venue is new to diving, but this session itself is a routine advancement round—one of several semifinal stages athletes navigate.

demand.explanation: Semifinal commands higher ticket interest than preliminary ($64–$554 range) and closer proximity to medal stakes, though without direct medal implications demand remains moderate. Expected to sell steadily as Games scarcity sustains baseline interest.

scorecard.overall: A solid semifinal session with merit for spectators seeking closer proximity to podium-contending athletes, but morning timing and non-medal status keep it from premium tier. The pressure and precision required for advancement offer compelling competition even without gold-medal stakes.

significance.explanation: Women's 3m springboard final crowns the first individual diving gold medal of the 2028 program. Medal-deciding stakes in a marquee round elevate significance, though springboard ranks below the highest-tier marquee events globally.

experience.explanation: Afternoon session (3:30 PM–4:45 PM) with high-energy sport and open-air venue in pleasant California light. demand.explanation: Price ceiling rises to $316.28 reflecting proximity to medal matches and semifinal status. Fans pursuing final-four competition will compete for these tickets, and Games presale scarcity will drive notable demand. Still somewhat easier to obtain than evening gold-medal matches.

scorecard.overall: Men's semifinals deliver high competitive stakes with potential rematch drama between Paris 2024 medalists. Strong demand from fans seeking medal-contender action, though the afternoon slot and secondary status to evening gold matches keep this below the top-tier sessions.

significance.explanation: Men's gold medal match crowns the tournament champion and determines bronze-medal positions. This is the culminating event of men's Rugby Sevens with the highest possible medal stakes and ceremonial significance.

experience.explanation: Evening matches (7:00–9:30 PM) under Carson Stadium's lights deliver peak atmosphere and crowd energy. The compact venue amplifies roars as medals are decided, and winners celebrate meters from the stands, creating unforgettable live spectacle.

starPower.explanation: Paris 2024 gold medalist France seeks to defend their title, while silver medalist Argentina and bronze medalist New Zealand aim to improve their finishes. The session concentrates the men's tournament's highest-profile talent and medal contenders in the final podium moments.

uniqueness.explanation: Men's Rugby Sevens gold-medal final during the 2028 Games is a marquee sporting moment occurring once every four years. The pairing of medal-deciding stakes, Carson Stadium's prominence, and prime-time evening window creates a rare, non-repeatable event.

demand.explanation: Highest price ceiling ($477.52) reflects gold-medal championship status and prime-time evening window. This is the most sought-after men's sevens session, expected to reach immediate sell-out as fans pursue the tournament's climactic moment.

scorecard.overall: The men's gold-medal final stands as the tournament's premier session, commanding top-tier demand and delivering the sport's highest stakes in prime-time venue spectacle. This is the marquee men's sevens event of the 2028 Games, blending world-class competition with unforgettable ceremonial atmosphere.

significance.explanation: A preliminary qualification day where athletes earn advancement points but no medals are awarded. Kite's multi-day format spreads stakes across the series rather than concentrating them in this opening session.

experience.explanation: Strong beach venue with ocean views and accessible sandy-shore spectating, though a 2:00 PM start on Sunday afternoon lacks the prime-time energy of evening finals. The six-hour window allows extended viewing of foiling boards at high speed.

starPower.explanation: Paris 2024 medalists like Valentin Bontus and Lauriane Nolot are likely competitors in qualification rounds where top athletes establish early momentum, though peak star power concentrates in the finals.

uniqueness.explanation: A standard qualification-round session in a well-established discipline; the venue is new to the Games but the format is routine across multiple days of similar preliminaries. This is one of several kite qualification sessions rather than a singular event.

demand.explanation: Entry-level pricing at $28 reflects the preliminary stage and daytime slot, making this easier to book relative to medal finals. Still a 2028 Games session on an attractive beach venue, but without the competition or scarcity of evening championship rounds.

scorecard.overall: A baseline qualifier that opens kite competition with accessible spectating and lower demand. It carries real athletic stakes for advancement but falls short of the medal-deciding drama and premium pricing that drive harder-to-get seats.

significance.explanation: A double-header of early qualification days where windsurfing launches its series and kite racers pursue day-two advancement. No medals are decided; both events remain in preliminary rounds building points.

experience.explanation: Belmont Shore's beach setting and extended six-hour evening window support dual-event spectating with views of foiling technology creating dynamic water action. Afternoon-to-dusk timing captures better light and energy than pure daytime, though still below prime-time finals atmosphere.

starPower.explanation: Paris 2024 medalists including Luuc van Opzeeland and Sharon Kantor appear in windsurfing qualifiers where top competitors race from the outset. Celebrity factor remains diffuse across a broad field in early rounds rather than concentrated among medal contenders.

uniqueness.explanation: A routine dual-qualification session combining day one of one discipline with day two of another. The double-header structure is practical but not rare; multiple such sessions occur during the preliminary week at this venue.

demand.explanation: Uniform $28 pricing and preliminary-round status place this among the easier-to-access sailing sessions. The extended afternoon window and dual-event format broaden appeal, but advancement-focused competition rather than medal races limits scarcity-driven demand.

scorecard.overall: A solid preliminary double-header with appealing beach-venue spectating and foiling-technology action. Demand and scarcity remain modest compared to finals, and the early qualification context means stakes are accumulated rather than decided.

significance.explanation: Windsurfing day two and kite's final qualification day both remain in the preliminary phase where points accumulate. Kite qualifiers conclude the seeding process, but medals are not awarded until the subsequent finals session.

experience.explanation: Belmont Shore continues to offer excellent beach spectating with Pacific ocean backdrop and close foiling-board action visible from the shore. Tuesday afternoon timing maintains decent light and energy, though still daytime-weighted rather than evening prime.

starPower.explanation: Top-ranked 2024 medalists including Lauriane Nolot and Luuc van Opzeeland are in the field as kite enters its decisive qualification day and windsurfing advances through day two. Competition remains world-class, though the focus is seeding rather than medals.

uniqueness.explanation: Another routine mid-series qualification day where kite concludes preliminaries and windsurfing progresses through qualifiers. The session is functional in the competitive schedule but structurally similar to prior and subsequent qualification rounds.

demand.explanation: Uniform $28 pricing maintains entry-level access; kite's final qualifier may draw slightly more interest as the last chance to qualify for finals, but it is still a preliminary round without medal stakes.

scorecard.overall: A mid-week qualifier where both disciplines close in on finals selection. Spectating quality remains strong, but the session functions as a stepping stone rather than a destination event, and pricing reflects its preliminary status.

significance.explanation: Formula Kite awards its gold, silver, and bronze medals in finals with a compressed two-race format where the top 10 compete under single-point scoring. Medals are decided; windsurfing day three qualifiers continue earlier rounds alongside.

experience.explanation: Kite finals deliver high-stakes tactical racing visible from the Long Beach shore with dramatic foiling boards and compressed competitive drama. Wednesday afternoon timing is solid, though a 2:00 PM start falls short of evening prime-time energy that would elevate the live atmosphere.

starPower.explanation: Kite finals feature top-ranked Paris 2024 medalists like Toni Vodišek and Lauriane Nolot competing for gold against other world-level competitors. The medal round concentrates globally recognized athletes, though kite remains less universally famous than marquee sports.

uniqueness.explanation: Kite's gold-medal finals are a once-per-Games event at Belmont Shore, pairing a medal-deciding round with a new-to-2028 competition venue. The format is not unprecedented, but the combination of medal stakes and a fresh Games setting carries special weight.

demand.explanation: Price escalates to $104.19, reflecting the medal-round status and competitive drama. Kite's emerging profile and daytime afternoon slot limit demand slightly versus evening marquee finals, but the combination of medals and a premiere beach venue drives solid ticket competition.

scorecard.overall: Kite's medal finals anchor this session with high competitive stakes and star power, elevated by mid-tier Games pricing and solid venue appeal. The afternoon timing and kite's niche profile prevent it from reaching top-tier demand, but medals and world-class competition establish it as a clear step above qualifiers.

significance.explanation: Windsurfing awards gold and silver medals for both men's and women's iQFoil, with top-10 competitors racing two single-point finals that decide podiums. Medals are conclusive; this is the climax of the three-day qualification series.

experience.explanation: Windsurfing finals at Belmont Shore present condensed championship action with foiling boards crossing decisive finish lines against the Pacific backdrop. The three-hour window concentrates intensity compared to earlier sessions. Thursday afternoon start is pleasant but not evening prime-time.

starPower.explanation: Paris 2024 champions Sharon Kantor and Luuc van Opzeeland are likely competitors in these finals, alongside other world-top-10 athletes. Windsurfing's medal round brings together the highest-ranked discipline competitors for the climactic races.

uniqueness.explanation: Windsurfing's gold-medal finals are a once-per-Games pairing of iQFoil championship racing with the beach venue of Belmont Shore. The format is established, but the specific combination of dual-gender medal finals at this location is singular within the 2028 Games schedule.

demand.explanation: Medal-round pricing of $104.19 reflects championship stakes, and windsurfing's longer Games heritage and broader athletic profile generate stronger scarcity than kite. The shorter three-hour window and daytime afternoon slot moderate demand slightly versus evening finals at major venues.

scorecard.overall: Windsurfing's gold-medal finals deliver world-class competition and athletic drama with established stars competing for the first board-event podiums. Premium pricing and medal stakes drive solid demand, though the daytime slot and specialized sport profile keep it a tier below the marquee evening championships at flagship venues.

significance.explanation: Opening day of qualification series sets the field but carries no medal stakes. Consistency across multiple heats determines advancement, with all meaningful drama deferred to finals later in the week.

experience.explanation: Afternoon slot (2–8 PM) on a Sunday offers decent viewing conditions and natural wind building through the day, though a six-hour marathon prelim lacks the compressed intensity of a medal race. Waterfront venue and sail traffic provide visual spectacle despite industrial harbor setting.

starPower.explanation: Top-ranked dinghy sailors from around the world are competing, but qualification series draws the full entry field rather than a narrowed medal contender pool. World-class athletes are present, though not yet concentrated in a spotlight moment.

uniqueness.explanation: First dinghy qualifier of the regatta marks the opening of the sailing program, but it remains a routine day-one qualification round—one of many heats across a 12-day window. The Port of Los Angeles setting is novel for sailing but this session itself is not a once-in-a-lifetime pairing.

demand.explanation: Lowest ticket price of the sailing slate ($28) reflects the preliminary round and early-week positioning. Qualification series attracts sailing enthusiasts and supporter bases but faces lower competition to book than medal finals or later-round sessions.

scorecard.overall: A foundational session that kicks off the dinghy program with world-class racers in a scenic but industrial harbor setting. Meaningful for competitors and sailing aficionados, but the lack of medal stakes and abundance of similar qualification heats across the week keep it at the baseline Games experience—accessible and worthwhile, though not a standout draw.

significance.explanation: This is a preliminary qualification session across three classes — no medals are decided, and advancement through the series remains the primary stake. However, these opening days establish positioning within the fleet and carry real competitive weight for athletes fighting for favorable standings.

experience.explanation: Six hours of continuous racing action across three classes at a historic waterfront venue offers solid engagement, with foiling Nacra 17s and tactical 470 dinghy racing providing visual variety. The afternoon-to-evening window captures better light than morning, though harbor-side industrial surroundings lack the iconic backdrop of some marquee venues.

starPower.explanation: Qualification rounds draw world-class sailors and national champions, though globally recognized household names are less likely to have high profile exposure at this stage. The 470 and Nacra 17 fleets include elite competitors, but stardom in sailing builds through medal-round visibility rather than early-series prominence.

uniqueness.explanation: Sailing returns to the 2028 Games after Paris 2024, and the dinghy and multihull format pairing at Port of Los Angeles reflects the revised class structure. However, this is one of multiple qualification-series sessions across the two-week window — the venue and multi-class format provide novelty within the sport, but the session itself follows a standard preliminary structure.

demand.explanation: Affordable entry pricing at $28 and preliminary-round status position this below marquee finals, though Games scarcity and the afternoon-evening window support solid ticket interest. Sailing draws a dedicated global fanbase, and the combined three-class slate may attract enthusiasts planning to sample multiple racing formats across the Games window.

scorecard.overall: A working session that showcases sailing's technical appeal — six hours of fleet racing with tactical depth and modern foiling spectacle — at a historic waterfront venue with solid access. Stakes are earned rather than decisive, positioning this as a strong introduction to the sport for attendees rather than a must-see medal showdown, but the variety of classes and continuous action elevate it above a single-fleet preliminary.

significance.explanation: Four classes racing simultaneously means high volume of competition, but all remain in qualification series format. No medals are decided; points accumulate across multiple days toward finals spots. The session is tactically critical for athletes but lacks medal-round stakes.

experience.explanation: Six racecourses simultaneously create a sensory spectacle of sail traffic and overlapping competition, with announcers juggling updates across disciplines. Afternoon timing and building harbor breeze enhance viewing conditions. Crowded schedule means busier spectator energy, though the industrial port backdrop remains utilitarian rather than iconic.

starPower.explanation: Skiff introduction brings Spain's Paris 2024 gold crew, New Zealand's silver medalists, and the American bronze duo into the water alongside other world-ranked competitors. Top talent is present but spread thinly across four concurrent classes in early rounds.

uniqueness.explanation: First appearance of skiff classes adds novelty within the sailing program, but the session is still a mid-series qualification day. The multi-class format is busy but not a singular defining moment of the regatta—similar overlapping days will follow.

demand.explanation: Same $28 price as opening day, reflecting qualification status. Ticket availability likely easier than medal finals, though the spectacle of six racecourses and returning Paris 2024 medalists may drive slightly elevated interest among sailing followers tracking top competitors.

scorecard.overall: A crowded, energetic session showcasing the breadth of the sailing program and introducing skiff racing to the regatta. The simultaneous action and presence of Paris 2024 medallists add appeal, but the qualification-round format and dispersed focus across four classes prevent it from being a must-see moment. Strong for sailing enthusiasts; solid for general Games ticket-holders seeking visual dynamism.

significance.explanation: Men's and Women's Dinghy finals are gold-medal races with doubled points determining podium finishes. Two medals are decided, marking the first medals of the sailing program. Concurrent skiff and mixed boat qualification rounds add supporting drama but are subsidiary to the dinghy finals.

experience.explanation: Afternoon slot and prime wind window create ideal racing conditions for compressed, high-stakes dinghy finals. Ten-boat fields generate tight tactical racing and immediate podium celebrations dockside. Multi-class activity fills the harbor, though dinghy finalists remain the session's focal point.

starPower.explanation: Dinghy finals draw the ten best-qualified sailors from each class—a concentrated pool of top-ranked competitors fighting for medals. These are the world's premier single-handed and one-woman sailors, though not all will be globally household names outside sailing circles.

uniqueness.explanation: First medal races of the sailing program at the Port of Los Angeles carry significance, but dinghy finals are a standard format at Games regattas. The moment is notable within sailing history rather than a once-in-a-lifetime venue-sport pairing; multiple medal races follow in the week.

demand.explanation: Price increase to $104.19 reflects medal-round status and first opportunity to witness gold medals in sailing. Finals drive higher demand than prelims, though non-marquee sport status and afternoon timing mean demand remains modest relative to marquee Games events like basketball or track finals.

scorecard.overall: The sailing program's first medal moment, delivering two gold-medal races and concentrated talent in a wind-favorable afternoon slot. The waterfront venue and dinghy finale create a solid spectator experience, though sailing's niche popularity and preliminary position within the broader Games calendar keep it from commanding premium scarcity. A strong draw for sailing followers and worth attending if interested in the sport, but not a tickets-at-any-cost finale.

significance.explanation: Two gold-medal races decide championships in Mixed Dinghy (470) and Mixed Multihull (Nacra 17), with doubled points. High-stakes finals involving crew synergy and foiling boat handling. Concurrent skiff qualification maintains supporting competition momentum.

experience.explanation: Afternoon timing with building sun and breeze creates optimal racing conditions. Multihull foils and mixed crew synchronized maneuvers deliver dynamic, visually striking action. Immediate podium celebrations dockside enhance the live spectacle. Two consecutive medals and back-to-back dramatic finishes concentrate emotion and energy.

starPower.explanation: Mixed dinghy and multihull finals attract top-seeded crew pairs from around the world competing for gold. While not individually household names, the caliber of world-championship sailors is exceptionally high. Skiff qualification races include Paris 2024 medalists working toward their own finals.

uniqueness.explanation: Two consecutive medal races on the same afternoon—Mixed Dinghy and Nacra 17 back-to-back—create a distinct session within the week-long regatta. The foiling multihull format is visually novel and technically advanced. However, these remain standard medal-race formats at Games regattas, not once-in-a-lifetime pairings.

demand.explanation: Second-day medal pricing ($104.19) and two gold-medal races drive elevated demand. Mixed formats and the visual spectacle of foiling multihulls attract broader sailing interest. Demand remains below marquee marquee-sport finals but distinctly higher than qualification rounds.

scorecard.overall: A strong sailing session delivering two consecutive medals, foiling multihull drama, and optimal afternoon wind. The visual dynamism and concentrated medal stakes make this the week's most compelling sailing experience. Mixed dinghy and multihull racing showcases skill and speed, though sailing's niche status within the broader Games prevents this from commanding the universal scarcity of basketball or athletics finals. Highly sought after by sailing enthusiasts and well worth a ticket if available.

significance.explanation: Two gold-medal races determine final sailing championships—Men's and Women's Skiff (49er and 49erFX) closing out the entire 12-day regatta. Skiff racing is the most physically demanding discipline, and these finals cap the program with the sport's most spectacular class. Maximum stakes and dramatic finish to sailing's 2028 Games narrative.

experience.explanation: Compressed three-hour afternoon window with prime wind delivers intense, fast-paced action. Skiff crews hike on wires, execute rapid spinnaker work, and plane downwind at 20+ knots—acrobatic and visually dynamic racing. Festive closing atmosphere as the sailing community gathers for final medals and immediate celebrations.

starPower.explanation: Spain's Paris 2024 Men's Skiff gold crew is projected to return alongside New Zealand's silver medalists and the American bronze duo. Skiff finals concentrate the sport's top-ranked two-person crews—exceptionally high global caliber of competitive sailors with international profiles within the sailing world.

uniqueness.explanation: The final medals of the 2028 sailing program delivered by skiff racing—the sport's most demanding and visually spectacular class—creates a distinct and memorable conclusion. Paris 2024 medalists returning for a medal race finish adds historical continuity. This specific pairing of regatta-closing moment with skiff drama is once-per-Games.

demand.explanation: Final-day sailing and closing-session prestige drive elevated ticket demand relative to earlier rounds. Price ceiling remains $104.19 (consistent with other medal races), indicating moderate price ceiling relative to marquee sports, but Friday afternoon positioning in the Games calendar and closing-program status amplify scarcity and seeking behavior.

scorecard.overall: The sailing program's grand finale, pairing the sport's most physically demanding and visually dynamic class with the final medals of the regatta. Spain's title defense, New Zealand and USA medal crews, and the spectacle of skiff foils and high-speed crew maneuvers create a compelling close. Friday afternoon timing and closing-event prestige enhance desirability. While sailing remains a niche sport, this is the most sought-after sailing session of the Games—a genuine highlight for the sailing community and a strong draw for Games attendees seeking dynamic competition.

significance.explanation: Opening gold medals for rifle and pistol on the Games' first competition day. Two medal events (women's and men's 10m Air Rifle) decided in a single session with immediate podium implications.

experience.explanation: Strong afternoon-to-evening window in a purpose-built indoor venue with excellent sightlines and 13,500 capacity. The quiet, focused atmosphere and visible precision mechanics create an intimate competition feel despite the large venue.

starPower.explanation: Medal round attracts the world's elite 10m Air Rifle shooters, though the discipline lacks mainstream global recognition. Top-ranked competitors from China, USA, and Europe will contend, representing generations of dominant programs.

uniqueness.explanation: Combination of opening-day position (rifle/pistol claim first medals of the Games) and the specialized technical spectacle of 10m Air Rifle at an indoor facility creates a distinctive debut moment for shooting in 2028.

demand.explanation: Opening-day prestige and dual-medal structure drive moderate-to-strong demand. Shooting's niche global appeal and affordable price ceiling ($104) make tickets more accessible than marquee sports, though Games scarcity ensures broad interest.

scorecard.overall: A significant opening statement for rifle and pistol with two immediate medal decisions and strong technical appeal. The early-Games position and first-medals-awarded status push this above routine finals, though the sport's specialist audience keeps it from elite prestige levels.

significance.explanation: Two gold medals decided (women's and men's 10m Air Pistol) on day two of competition, maintaining shooting's medal momentum. Pistol events claim podium stakes immediately after rifle's opening.

experience.explanation: Afternoon-to-evening session in the same Long Beach indoor facility with favorable lighting and crowd proximity. The one-handed technical demands create distinctive body mechanics visible from all angles in tiered seating.

starPower.explanation: Global elite pistol shooters compete for medals; the discipline has produced consistent international champions from Asia, Europe, and the Americas. Recognition remains limited outside shooting circles despite top athletes' decades of dominance.

uniqueness.explanation: Second consecutive opening-week medal session for rifle/pistol, with pistol's distinct technical profile (one-handed, higher shooting rate) differentiating it from day one. The consecutive dual-event opening gives the discipline rare prominence.

demand.explanation: Same presale dynamics as SHO01: opening-week prestige and dual medals drive interest, but niche appeal and consistent low price point mean tickets remain more obtainable than marquee events despite Games scarcity.

scorecard.overall: Another high-significance medal session maintaining shooting's opening-week momentum with a technical sport experience that benefits from indoor venue proximity. Comparable to SHO01 in stakes and accessibility, anchoring the early competitive schedule.

significance.explanation: First day of skeet qualification establishes the field but carries no medal stakes—advancement stakes exist for competitors, though medals remain two rounds away. This is a preliminary round where the world's best shooters compete for position.

experience.explanation: Morning and early-afternoon session at an open-air clay center with unobstructed sightlines and audible shots, but lacks the prime-time energy and concentrated crowd intensity of medal rounds. The natural landscape provides scenic appeal typical of shooting venues.

starPower.explanation: World-class skeet shooters compete including national champions and established medalists, though at the qualification stage top names are not yet concentrated in single matches. Every competitor here is a Games-level athlete.

uniqueness.explanation: Day 1 of a multi-day qualification follows standard format with no structural changes from Paris 2024. While at a Southern California venue, this is one of multiple preliminary rounds across the shotgun program—not a once-in-a-lifetime pairing.

demand.explanation: Lowest price ceiling and earliest session time relative to medal rounds place this in the accessible tier, though Games scarcity ensures presale traction. Easier to book than finals, but still a 2028 Summer Games ticket.

scorecard.overall: A foundational qualification session that establishes the skeet field. It showcases world-class shooting in a pleasant open-air setting with solid sightlines, but without medal stakes or prime-time scheduling, it lands at the baseline tier for the shooting program.

significance.explanation: Four medals awarded in a single session across mixed team rifle and pistol events. The unified four-team final format (2028 change from Paris) concentrates competitive drama and multiple podium moments.

experience.explanation: Afternoon-to-evening session showcasing two distinct technical disciplines back-to-back within hours. Spectators witness standing rifle positions transitioning to one-handed pistol stance, offering the broadest variety in the shooting program.

starPower.explanation: Mixed team format brings together verified gold-medal contenders from both disciplines competing for their nations. Top programs field star-tier shooters even in team rounds, though individual names lack mainstream recognition.

uniqueness.explanation: Mixed team events with the new unified four-team final format create a format variation that distinguishes this session from individual events. The back-to-back rifle-pistol structure offers variety not found elsewhere in the shooting schedule.

demand.explanation: Four medals and early-Games timing drive moderate demand above typical qualifiers. Mixed team appeal and format novelty (unified finals) attract interest, though shooting's price ceiling and audience size keep demand below marquee sport standards.

scorecard.overall: A mid-schedule medal session with four immediate podium decisions and format variety that showcases shooting's technical range. Strong competitive significance and diverse technical appeal position it solidly, though specialist audience confines it from top-tier presale velocity.

significance.explanation: Men's and women's skeet finals award gold and silver medals with competitive stakes throughout. Wraps qualification day 2 into one session, consolidating two major events with medal outcomes in a single venue day.

experience.explanation: Extended day combining morning qualification and afternoon medal rounds at an outdoor venue with good spectator positioning. Finals intensity and afternoon timing provide higher energy than prelims, though still daytime hours at a clay shooting facility rather than an indoor arena.

starPower.explanation: Skeet finals concentrate the world's top shooters in head-to-head elimination format. Medal-round competitors are established champions and contenders, with household names likely among the eight finalists.

uniqueness.explanation: First skeet final of the Games at Whittier Narrows, with the new eight-competitor format from 2026 onward providing a structural update from Paris 2024. Medal-round finals are significant events, though skeet itself is not a Games flagship.

demand.explanation: Medal-round status and expanded eight-finalist format drive stronger ticket interest than qualification. Price ceiling remains modest relative to marquee sports, but demand should exceed prelims significantly.

scorecard.overall: A dual-session package delivering skeet medal outcomes with strong competitive drama and concentrated star power. The extended day and afternoon medal rounds elevate it above qualification, though the daytime slot and clay shooting context prevent it from reaching prime-time intensity.

significance.explanation: Gold medal decided for Men's 50m Rifle 3 Positions with the 2028 standing-only final format. Single medal event with high technical stakes as the standing position creates an elevated difficulty bar compared to historical Paris format.

starPower.explanation: Elite men's rifle shooters compete for a standing-position gold at a major Games. The discipline attracts world-class athletes though few achieve mainstream recognition; top contenders represent established rifle powers with proven credentials.

uniqueness.explanation: The 2028 standing-only final format (change from Paris' position rotation) creates a notable technical shift that adds distinctiveness. Morning timing and the extended 50-meter range setup offer a different spectator experience than earlier rapid-fire pistol events.

demand.explanation: Mid-week morning slot and single-medal stakes reduce presale urgency versus opening-day dual-medal sessions. Shooting enthusiasts and local spectators can access the event more readily than earlier finals, though Games scarcity maintains baseline demand across all sessions.

scorecard.overall: A medal session with format innovation (standing-only final) providing technical interest, though morning timing and single-event structure temper both live experience intensity and competitive presale velocity compared to opening shooting events.

significance.explanation: Gold medal for Women's 50m Rifle 3 Positions with the standing-only final format. Single medal with same technical elevation as SHO06, maintaining high stakes across the 50m rifle program.

experience.explanation: Morning session timing (9:30 AM) lacks prime-time energy, though the arena's controlled indoor environment and extended 50-meter range visibility create optimal conditions for observing technical precision. Waterfront venue location offers cultural amenities pre/post-competition.

starPower.explanation: World-class women's rifle shooters with Paris and prior Games pedigree compete for 50m gold. International representation is strong though individual names remain specialist-audience territory; top contenders hail from traditional rifle powerhouses.

uniqueness.explanation: Parallel format to SHO06 with the 2028 standing-only final variant. The women's entry offers technical distinctiveness through format change, though the morning scheduling and single-medal structure limit uniqueness versus opening-day dual-medal sessions.

demand.explanation: Mid-week morning position and single-medal structure keep demand moderate relative to opening shooting gold medals. The event's prestige remains solid, but daytime scheduling and specialist appeal allow more ticket accessibility than evening finals.

scorecard.overall: A medal session with format innovation matching the men's 50m event, providing technical appeal though morning timing and single-event stakes limit both spectator atmosphere intensity and presale competition versus shooting's opening week.

significance.explanation: Trap qualification day 1 is a preliminary round establishing the field without medals at stake. Competitors earn advancement through performance, but medal outcomes remain multiple rounds ahead.

experience.explanation: Morning-to-afternoon session across multiple trap fields with simultaneous viewing and the distinctive sound of shotgun fire across the recreation area. Daytime timing and preliminary-round pacing lack the intensity arc of finals, though the open-air setting provides scenic context.

starPower.explanation: World-class trap shooters including established medalists and national champions, though qualification stages don't concentrate star power as much as finals do. Still, every competitor represents elite-level shooting.

uniqueness.explanation: Trap qualification day 1 follows unchanged Paris 2024 format across a multi-day structure. This is one of several preliminary rounds in the shooting program with no structural novelty.

demand.explanation: Qualification slot with modest pricing and morning-to-afternoon timing places this in the accessible category relative to medal rounds. Games scarcity still drives presale interest, but this is easier to book than finals.

scorecard.overall: A standard qualification opener for trap shooting at a premier clay center. Strong competitor base and scenic venue provide solid viewing, but lack of medal stakes and preliminary-round format keep it at baseline tier within the shooting program.

significance.explanation: Preliminary qualification round (precision stage only) with no medal decision. Advancement stakes are earned through qualification threshold rather than podium positions; this session determines finalists for tomorrow's rapid-fire and medal rounds.

experience.explanation: Morning preliminary session (9:30 AM–noon) in the Long Beach venue creates baseline spectator conditions. starPower.explanation: World-level women's 25m pistol competitors appear in qualification, though the format (slow-fire precision) and preliminary timing mean even top-ranked shooters lack the spotlight intensity of medal rounds. Athletes are elite but unheralded globally.

uniqueness.explanation: Routine preliminary qualification session for an established event with no format changes announced. While the precision stage represents a technical discipline, the session itself follows standard qualification structure repeated multiple times across the shooting program.

demand.explanation: Early-morning preliminary timing and no-medals structure place this at baseline demand for shooting events. Specialist shooting enthusiasts will attend to watch technique development, but the session lacks the prestige, drama, or time-slot energy that drives broader Games interest.

scorecard.overall: A baseline preliminary session offering technical shooting spectacle but limited by morning timing and qualification-only stakes. Accessible for specialist audiences and casual attendees seeking off-peak Games tickets, though lacking the drama or prestige of medal rounds.

significance.explanation: Men's and women's trap finals award medals with elimination stakes throughout. Consolidates day 2 qualification and medal rounds in a single extended session, delivering two gold-medal outcomes.

experience.explanation: Full-day session mixing morning qualification and afternoon medal rounds at an outdoor clay facility with good spectator positioning and high atmospheric intensity. Finals drama and late-afternoon timing provide stronger energy than prelims, though still a daytime shooting environment.

starPower.explanation: Trap finals concentrate the world's elite shooters in head-to-head medal rounds. The eight-finalist format includes reigning champions and globally-recognized names from previous Games cycles.

uniqueness.explanation: Trap medal rounds at Whittier Narrows with the expanded eight-finalist format from 2026. Medal-day status is significant, though trap finals remain one of multiple shooting events without unique venue or format pairings.

demand.explanation: Medal-round status and extended day combining qualification closure with gold-medal climax drive strong ticket demand relative to prelims. Price ceiling should exceed qualification, positioning this in the mid-tier for shooting scarcity.
